title: CT Head — Basic Interpretation
oneLiner: Urgent CT head without contrast for acute neurological symptoms — rule out haemorrhage, mass effect, herniation and guide neurosurgical or medical management.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute extra‑axial haemorrhage with mass effect** (extradural / subdural / subarachnoid) — look for midline shift >5 mm, obliterated basal cisterns, effacement of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle. **Single finding:** any asymmetry of the lateral ventricles or basal cisterns (the "swirl sign" in an extradural haematoma = active bleeding).
- **Cerebellar haemorrhage or infarct with hydrocephalus** — look for effacement of the fourth ventricle, dilatation of temporal horns (early hydrocephalus). **Single finding:** loss of the normal "Mickey Mouse" shape of the midbrain (early brainstem compression).
- **Large acute ischaemic stroke with early herniation** — look for loss of grey‑white matter differentiation >1/3 of the MCA territory, sulcal effacement, hyperdense MCA sign. **Single finding:** midline shift >1 cm or unilateral uncal herniation (effaced ipsilateral suprasellar cistern and midbrain rotation).
sections.Presentation:
- Sudden onset severe headache ("thunderclap") ± vomiting (SAH)
- New focal neurological deficit / weakness / speech disturbance (stroke / ICH)
- Reduced GCS following trauma (head injury)
- Seizure with post‑ictal focal signs or persistent drowsiness
- Signs of raised ICP: Cushing’s triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing), papillodema (late)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Traumatic ICH** (extradural, subdural, intraparenchymal) — history of trauma; skull fracture may be seen.
- **Spontaneous ICH** (hypertensive, amyloid, vascular malformation) — no trauma; deep (basal ganglia/thalamus) vs lobar.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** — blood in basal cisterns, sylvian fissures; no trauma.
- **Acute ischaemic stroke** — no blood; subtle early signs (loss of insular ribbon, lentiform nucleus obscuration, hyperdense artery sign).
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** — no acute changes on CT; may show meningeal enhancement post‑contrast (not routine).
- **Mass lesion (tumour, abscess)** — chronic history; perilesional oedema, ring enhancement (if contrast used).
- **"Splitter":** Blood vs No blood on CT — this is the single most critical bifurcation. If no blood, consider ischaemic stroke, early ischaemic changes, or false negative SAH (need LP if clinical suspicion).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** GCS, pupillary response, limb strength (MRC), gross visual fields; history of anticoagulant/antiplatelet use; time of last known well (for thrombolysis window).
- **Bloods:** FBC (platelets), PT/APTT, INR (anticoagulation), sodium (hyponatraemia in SAH), glucose (rule out hypoglycaemia mimicking stroke).
- **Imaging:** Non‑contrast CT head — first‑line. CT perfusion if trying to distinguish infarct core vs penumbra (local protocol). CT angiogram if suspected aneurysm or dissection. MRI brain if posterior fossa or small infarcts.
- **Special tests:** Lumbar puncture if CT negative but high suspicion for SAH (xanthochromia). ECG (troponin, arrhythmias — neurogenic stress cardiomyopathy in SAH).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE head injury guideline (CG176) — CT head within 1 hour if GCS <13 at any point, or if loss of consciousness with amnesia plus risk factors (anticoagulation, age >65, dangerous mechanism, vomit >1, seizure). For stroke: BEFAST screening and CT within 1 hour if within 4.5-hour thrombolysis window.
- **Never‑miss test:** Coagulation screen (INR, APTT, platelets) in any patient with a CT showing ICH — reversing anticoagulation is time‑critical.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Call neurosurgery (or stroke team) if mass effect, ICH, SAH, or early herniation signs.
  - ABC – secure airway if GCS ≤8 or declining; maintain SpO₂ >94%, normocapnia (PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa) – avoid hypercapnia (vasodilation worsens ICP).
  - Reverse anticoagulation (warfarin: vitamin K + PCC; DOACs: specific reversal agents if available – [check local policy]).
  - Treat seizure (lorazepam 2–4 mg IV; loading with levetiracetam or phenytoin if recurrent).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Neurological observations every 30–60 minutes (GCS, pupils, limb power) – early detection of herniation.
  - Maintain mean arterial pressure 80–90 mmHg for stroke/ICH (avoid hypotension); for SAH, may aim for higher MAP to prevent vasospasm.
  - CT follow‑up at 24h if trauma or if clinical deterioration.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:** Refer to neurosurgery for structural lesion; stroke specialist for ischaemic stroke; vascular neurosurgery ± interventional radiology for SAH (aneurysm clipping or coiling).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1–2 mL/kg/hour (avoid hypotonic fluids).
  - VTE prophylaxis: pneumatic calf compressors (avoid anticoagulation until ICH stability confirmed – usually 24–48h).
  - NBM until swallowing assessed or if reduced GCS.
  - Observations: NEWS2 + neuro observations q1h (initial) then q4h when stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Mannitol** (for raised ICP / herniation): 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 15–20 minutes — [check local policy] for dose and repeat interval.
- **Hypertonic saline** (3%): 150–250 mL IV bolus — [check local policy].
- **Levetiracetam** (seizure prophylaxis in ICH): 500–1500 mg IV BD — adjust for renal function (CrCl <50: reduce dose – [check local policy]).
- **Reversal of warfarin:**
  - Vitamin K: 5–10 mg slow IV.
  - Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC): 25–50 IU/kg IV (dose per INR – [check local policy]).
- **Reversal of DOACs:**
  - Dabigatran: idarucizumab 5 g IV.
  - Apixaban/rivaroxaban: andexanet alfa bolus + infusion – [check local policy].
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Mannitol contraindicated in anuria; hypertonic saline in severe hyponatraemia.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levetiracetam – reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** 
  - Mannitol — giving a repeat dose too early (risk of hypovolaemia and acute kidney injury). Always confirm that the first dose was effective (improved ICP) before repeating.
  - Reversal agents — miscalculating PCC dose based on INR can lead to over‑or under‑reversal.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Brain herniation (uncal, central, tonsillar), seizures (especially within 24h), re‑bleeding (if untreated coagulopathy), hydrocephalus (common in SAH), vasospasm (days 3–14 post‑SAH).
- **Chronic:** Residual neurological deficit (hemiparesis, aphasia, cognitive impairment), post‑traumatic epilepsy, chronic subdural hygroma, normal‑pressure hydrocephalus (months after SAH/trauma).
sections.Prognosis: - Extradural haematoma with rapid evacuation – excellent if conscious pre‑op. Intraparenchymal ICH >30 mL (supratentorial) or cerebellar ICH >3 cm – high mortality. SAH mortality ~25–40% (depends on WFNS grade and re‑bleed). Large MCA infarct >1/3 territory – malignant MCA syndrome with 60% mortality without decompressive hemicraniectomy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Compare left and right side systematically** – the most sensitive pattern‑recognition step for asymmetry (midline shift, effaced cistern, loss of ventricle).
- **Check bone windows** for acute skull fractures (especially if scalp haematoma is present) – often missed on brain windows.
- **Always look for the "hyperdense artery" sign** on non‑contrast CT (suggesting clot occluding MCA or basilar artery) – even if CT is otherwise normal, this indicates large‑vessel occlusion suitable for thrombectomy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't call a subdural haematoma an "acute" bleed if the blood is isodense** (subacute/chronic) – chronic SDH can be isodense to brain, look for effacement of sulci ± midline shift.
- **Never interpret a CT head without clinical context** – a small chronic subdural in an elderly patient on anticoagulants may be life‑threatening even if imaging appears unremarkable.
- **Don't miss early ischaemic changes** – check for loss of grey‑white differentiation in the insular ribbon (first sign of MCA infarction) and the lentiform nucleus.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening headache (especially if sudden/severe), new focal weakness, seizures, confusion, vomiting, drowsiness (GCS drop), or any visual disturbance.
- **Follow‑up:** 
  - If negative CT but high suspicion for SAH – arrange lumbar puncture within 12 hours (xanthochromia).
  - Neurology clinic review within 2 weeks if transient ischaemic symptoms (TIA) – CT head is only part of workup.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/consultant) immediately if:** GCS drops ≥2 points, new anisocoria (pupil asymmetry >1 mm), new focal motor deficit, seizure >5 minutes, or any sign of herniation on CT.
- **Call ICU/crash team if:** GCS ≤8, airway compromise, respiratory arrest, or rapidly declining consciousness.
- **Specific thresholds:** 
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (if sepsis concern)
  - GCS ≤12 with acute change from baseline
  - Any CT evidence of significant mass effect (midline shift >5 mm, obliterated basal cisterns)

rawMarkdown:
## CT Head — Basic Interpretation
**One-liner:** Urgent CT head without contrast for acute neurological symptoms — rule out haemorrhage, mass effect, herniation and guide neurosurgical or medical management.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute extra‑axial haemorrhage with mass effect** (extradural / subdural / subarachnoid) — look for midline shift >5 mm, obliterated basal cisterns, effacement of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle. **Single finding:** any asymmetry of the lateral ventricles or basal cisterns (the "swirl sign" in an extradural haematoma = active bleeding).
- **Cerebellar haemorrhage or infarct with hydrocephalus** — look for effacement of the fourth ventricle, dilatation of temporal horns (early hydrocephalus). **Single finding:** loss of the normal "Mickey Mouse" shape of the midbrain (early brainstem compression).
- **Large acute ischaemic stroke with early herniation** — look for loss of grey‑white matter differentiation >1/3 of the MCA territory, sulcal effacement, hyperdense MCA sign. **Single finding:** midline shift >1 cm or unilateral uncal herniation (effaced ipsilateral suprasellar cistern and midbrain rotation).

### Presentation
- Sudden onset severe headache ("thunderclap") ± vomiting (SAH)
- New focal neurological deficit / weakness / speech disturbance (stroke / ICH)
- Reduced GCS following trauma (head injury)
- Seizure with post‑ictal focal signs or persistent drowsiness
- Signs of raised ICP: Cushing’s triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing), papillodema (late)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Traumatic ICH** (extradural, subdural, intraparenchymal) — history of trauma; skull fracture may be seen.
- **Spontaneous ICH** (hypertensive, amyloid, vascular malformation) — no trauma; deep (basal ganglia/thalamus) vs lobar.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** — blood in basal cisterns, sylvian fissures; no trauma.
- **Acute ischaemic stroke** — no blood; subtle early signs (loss of insular ribbon, lentiform nucleus obscuration, hyperdense artery sign).
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** — no acute changes on CT; may show meningeal enhancement post‑contrast (not routine).
- **Mass lesion (tumour, abscess)** — chronic history; perilesional oedema, ring enhancement (if contrast used).
- **"Splitter":** Blood vs No blood on CT — this is the single most critical bifurcation. If no blood, consider ischaemic stroke, early ischaemic changes, or false negative SAH (need LP if clinical suspicion).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** GCS, pupillary response, limb strength (MRC), gross visual fields; history of anticoagulant/antiplatelet use; time of last known well (for thrombolysis window).
- **Bloods:** FBC (platelets), PT/APTT, INR (anticoagulation), sodium (hyponatraemia in SAH), glucose (rule out hypoglycaemia mimicking stroke).
- **Imaging:** Non‑contrast CT head — first‑line. CT perfusion if trying to distinguish infarct core vs penumbra (local protocol). CT angiogram if suspected aneurysm or dissection. MRI brain if posterior fossa or small infarcts.
- **Special tests:** Lumbar puncture if CT negative but high suspicion for SAH (xanthochromia). ECG (troponin, arrhythmias — neurogenic stress cardiomyopathy in SAH).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE head injury guideline (CG176) — CT head within 1 hour if GCS <13 at any point, or if loss of consciousness with amnesia plus risk factors (anticoagulation, age >65, dangerous mechanism, vomit >1, seizure). For stroke: BEFAST screening and CT within 1 hour if within 4.5-hour thrombolysis window.
- **Never‑miss test:** Coagulation screen (INR, APTT, platelets) in any patient with a CT showing ICH — reversing anticoagulation is time‑critical.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Call neurosurgery (or stroke team) if mass effect, ICH, SAH, or early herniation signs.
  - ABC – secure airway if GCS ≤8 or declining; maintain SpO₂ >94%, normocapnia (PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa) – avoid hypercapnia (vasodilation worsens ICP).
  - Reverse anticoagulation (warfarin: vitamin K + PCC; DOACs: specific reversal agents if available – [check local policy]).
  - Treat seizure (lorazepam 2–4 mg IV; loading with levetiracetam or phenytoin if recurrent).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Neurological observations every 30–60 minutes (GCS, pupils, limb power) – early detection of herniation.
  - Maintain mean arterial pressure 80–90 mmHg for stroke/ICH (avoid hypotension); for SAH, may aim for higher MAP to prevent vasospasm.
  - CT follow‑up at 24h if trauma or if clinical deterioration.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:** Refer to neurosurgery for structural lesion; stroke specialist for ischaemic stroke; vascular neurosurgery ± interventional radiology for SAH (aneurysm clipping or coiling).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1–2 mL/kg/hour (avoid hypotonic fluids).
  - VTE prophylaxis: pneumatic calf compressors (avoid anticoagulation until ICH stability confirmed – usually 24–48h).
  - NBM until swallowing assessed or if reduced GCS.
  - Observations: NEWS2 + neuro observations q1h (initial) then q4h when stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Mannitol** (for raised ICP / herniation): 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 15–20 minutes — [check local policy] for dose and repeat interval.
- **Hypertonic saline** (3%): 150–250 mL IV bolus — [check local policy].
- **Levetiracetam** (seizure prophylaxis in ICH): 500–1500 mg IV BD — adjust for renal function (CrCl <50: reduce dose – [check local policy]).
- **Reversal of warfarin:**
  - Vitamin K: 5–10 mg slow IV.
  - Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC): 25–50 IU/kg IV (dose per INR – [check local policy]).
- **Reversal of DOACs:**
  - Dabigatran: idarucizumab 5 g IV.
  - Apixaban/rivaroxaban: andexanet alfa bolus + infusion – [check local policy].
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Mannitol contraindicated in anuria; hypertonic saline in severe hyponatraemia.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levetiracetam – reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** 
  - Mannitol — giving a repeat dose too early (risk of hypovolaemia and acute kidney injury). Always confirm that the first dose was effective (improved ICP) before repeating.
  - Reversal agents — miscalculating PCC dose based on INR can lead to over‑or under‑reversal.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Brain herniation (uncal, central, tonsillar), seizures (especially within 24h), re‑bleeding (if untreated coagulopathy), hydrocephalus (common in SAH), vasospasm (days 3–14 post‑SAH).
- **Chronic:** Residual neurological deficit (hemiparesis, aphasia, cognitive impairment), post‑traumatic epilepsy, chronic subdural hygroma, normal‑pressure hydrocephalus (months after SAH/trauma).

### Prognosis
- Extradural haematoma with rapid evacuation – excellent if conscious pre‑op. Intraparenchymal ICH >30 mL (supratentorial) or cerebellar ICH >3 cm – high mortality. SAH mortality ~25–40% (depends on WFNS grade and re‑bleed). Large MCA infarct >1/3 territory – malignant MCA syndrome with 60% mortality without decompressive hemicraniectomy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Compare left and right side systematically** – the most sensitive pattern‑recognition step for asymmetry (midline shift, effaced cistern, loss of ventricle).
- **Check bone windows** for acute skull fractures (especially if scalp haematoma is present) – often missed on brain windows.
- **Always look for the "hyperdense artery" sign** on non‑contrast CT (suggesting clot occluding MCA or basilar artery) – even if CT is otherwise normal, this indicates large‑vessel occlusion suitable for thrombectomy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't call a subdural haematoma an "acute" bleed if the blood is isodense** (subacute/chronic) – chronic SDH can be isodense to brain, look for effacement of sulci ± midline shift.
- **Never interpret a CT head without clinical context** – a small chronic subdural in an elderly patient on anticoagulants may be life‑threatening even if imaging appears unremarkable.
- **Don't miss early ischaemic changes** – check for loss of grey‑white differentiation in the insular ribbon (first sign of MCA infarction) and the lentiform nucleus.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening headache (especially if sudden/severe), new focal weakness, seizures, confusion, vomiting, drowsiness (GCS drop), or any visual disturbance.
- **Follow‑up:** 
  - If negative CT but high suspicion for SAH – arrange lumbar puncture within 12 hours (xanthochromia).
  - Neurology clinic review within 2 weeks if transient ischaemic symptoms (TIA) – CT head is only part of workup.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/consultant) immediately if:** GCS drops ≥2 points, new anisocoria (pupil asymmetry >1 mm), new focal motor deficit, seizure >5 minutes, or any sign of herniation on CT.
- **Call ICU/crash team if:** GCS ≤8, airway compromise, respiratory arrest, or rapidly declining consciousness.
- **Specific thresholds:** 
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (if sepsis concern)
  - GCS ≤12 with acute change from baseline
  - Any CT evidence of significant mass effect (midline shift >5 mm, obliterated basal cisterns)
